units.title: Basics 1
units.description: Learn your very first Chinese words and sentences! Meet people, name foods, and start talking about what you eat and drink.
units.guidebook:
Welcome to your first Chinese lesson! 🎉

**Nouns have no articles** in Chinese. There is no "a," "an," or "the" — 男人 means both "a man" and "the man."

**Subject + 是 (shì) + Noun** is the basic "to be" sentence. 是 means "am/is/are." Example: 我是女孩 (I am a girl).

**Verbs don't conjugate.** 吃 (chī, to eat) and 喝 (hē, to drink) stay the same no matter who does the action. Just say: 我喝水 (I drink water).

**Word order** is usually Subject + Verb + Object, just like English: 我吃面包 (I eat bread).

**Tones matter!** Mandarin has 4 tones plus a neutral tone. The pinyin shows tones with marks (ā, á, ǎ, à). Practice them out loud — they change meanings!

Let's go! 🚀

units.title: Greetings
units.description: Learn essential Chinese greetings and polite expressions to start any conversation with confidence.
units.guidebook:
## Greetings in Chinese 🇨🇳

Chinese greetings are wonderfully simple! The most common hello is **你好** (nǐ hǎo), used in most situations. For a polite version, use **您好** (nín hǎo) when addressing elders or strangers.

**Good morning** is **早上好** (zǎoshang hǎo), **good evening** is **晚上好** (wǎnshang hǎo), and **good night** is **晚安** (wǎn'ān).

**Goodbye** has several forms: **再见** (zàijiàn) is standard, while **拜拜** (bàibài) is a casual borrowed form from English "bye-bye."

**Politeness matters!** Always say **请** (qǐng) for "please" and **谢谢** (xièxie) for "thank you." A polite **不客气** (bú kèqi) means "you're welcome."

**How are you?** is **你好吗？** (nǐ hǎo ma?) — the particle **吗** turns a statement into a yes/no question. A common reply is **我很好** (wǒ hěn hǎo), "I'm very well."

**Tip:** Chinese often drops pronouns, so **好** alone can mean "okay" or "good!"

units.title: Food
units.description: Learn essential food and drink vocabulary, and how to say what you want, eat, and drink in Chinese.
units.guidebook:
## Talking About Food in Chinese 🥢

In this unit, you'll learn the most common food and drink words you'll encounter every day.

**The verb 想 (xiǎng)** means "to want" and is super useful when ordering food or expressing cravings. Pair it with a noun: **我想吃苹果** (wǒ xiǎng chī píngguǒ) — "I want to eat an apple."

**Two key verbs to remember:**
- **吃 (chī)** = "to eat" — used for solid food
- **喝 (hē)** = "to drink" — used for liquids like 水, 茶, and 果汁

**Measure words matter!** Chinese uses measure words (量词) when counting nouns. For most food items, use **个 (gè)**: 一个鸡蛋 (yī gè jīdàn) — "one egg."

**Saying "I want...":**
- 我要咖啡 (wǒ yào kāfēi) — "I want coffee" (more direct, like ordering)
- 我想喝水 (wǒ xiǎng hē shuǐ) — "I want to drink water" (more polite)

**Tones tip:** 喝 (hē, 1st tone) and 吃 (chī, 1st tone) are both high and flat — easy to remember together!

exercises.translation: Please give me a glass of water.

units.title: Animals
units.description: Learn the names of common animals in Chinese and how to describe them with simple sentences.
units.guidebook:
## Animals in Chinese 🐾

In this unit, you'll learn the names of six common animals and how to make simple sentences about them.

### Key Grammar: Using 是 (shì) for "to be"
To say "It is a dog," use the structure:
**Subject + 是 + Noun**
- 这是狗。 (Zhè shì gǒu.) — This is a dog.

### Using 很 (hěn) for descriptions
To describe an animal, use:
**Subject + 很 + Adjective**
- 猫很小。 (Māo hěn xiǎo.) — The cat is very small.

### Measure word: 只 (zhī)
When counting animals, use the measure word **只**:
- 一只鸟 (yì zhī niǎo) — one bird

### Negation with 不 (bù)
To make a sentence negative, place **不** before the verb or adjective:
- 鱼不大。 (Yú bù dà.) — The fish is not big.

Practice combining these patterns to build longer sentences!

exercises.answer: The bird flies in the sky.

exercises.translation: The fish is in the water.

units.title: Travel
units.description: Learn essential Chinese vocabulary and phrases for navigating cities, transportation, hotels, and airports.
units.guidebook:
## Getting Around in Chinese 🗺️

This unit covers the essentials of travel in Chinese-speaking regions. You'll learn how to ask for directions and identify key places.

**Asking "Where is...?"**
The most useful phrase is **在哪里?** (zài nǎlǐ?), meaning "Where is it?" Simply place a noun before it:
- **酒店在哪里?** (jiǔdiàn zài nǎlǐ?) — Where is the hotel?
- **机场在哪里?** (jīchǎng zài nǎlǐ?) — Where is the airport?

**Using 在 (zài) for location**
The verb **在** means "to be located at." Structure: **Place + 在 + Location**
- **酒店在城市里** (jiǔdiàn zài chéngshì lǐ) — The hotel is in the city.

**Going places with 去 (qù)**
To say you're going somewhere, use **去** (qù, "to go"):
- **我去机场** (wǒ qù jīchǎng) — I go to the airport.

**Tones matter!** Mandarin has four tones that change word meaning entirely. Practice with the pinyin provided. 加油! (jiāyóu!) — Keep it up!

exercises.answer: I am going to the airport.
The taxi is in the city.
